ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for stir-fried ee fu noodles; A0033 (Noodle dishes) is the closest parent category available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried ee fu noodles; code omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried ee fu noodles at a hawker centre or restaurant is approximately one plate, weighing around 250 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Ee Fu Noodles — Energy and Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for 'Stir fried ee fu noodles' (cr_id F05060124). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — wheat noodles, boiled (analogous estimate for ee fu noodles)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ee fu noodles are pre-fried alkaline wheat noodles. No direct GI measurement for ee fu noodles exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 (medium) is estimated by analogy with wheat-based noodles reported in Atkinson et al. (2008), Am J Clin Nutr 87(1):214S–234S, which documents GI values of 52–65 for various wheat noodle preparations. The input record cited GI=58 from an embedded database; this value falls within the peer-reviewed range and is retained as a defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 13.25 × 2.5) / 100 = 19.2 for a 250g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 530 mg per 100 g, with low dietary fibre (0.83 g/100 g) and no significant potassium offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, as a 250 g serving delivers approximately 1325 mg sodium — more than half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) combined with low dietary fibre (0.83 g/100 g) from pre-fried refined wheat noodles promotes rapid postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to no more than 150 g, increase vegetable accompaniments to slow glucose absorption, and consider substituting with lower-GI noodle alternatives such as glass noodles or soba.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (530 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (7.49 g/100 g) including saturated fat, with pre-frying of noodles generating advanced glycation end products (AGEs).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation, request reduced seasoning sauces to lower sodium intake, and be aware that the pre-fried noodle base contributes AGEs that may promote vascular inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Ee Fu Noodles (炒伊麵, also known as Yee Mee or Yi Mian) is a popular Singaporean and Chinese hawker dish featuring soft, springy, pre-fried alkaline wheat noodles that are wok-tossed with mushrooms, vegetables, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. The noodles are distinctive for their golden-yellow colour and porous texture, which results from the deep-frying process during manufacture, allowing them to absorb sauces and seasonings readily. The dish delivers a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with a tender, slightly chewy bite. Per 100 g, it provides 145 kcal, 6.09 g protein, 7.49 g fat, and 13.25 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 530 mg. The medium glycemic index (GI 58) and low dietary fibre (0.83 g/100 g) mean blood glucose rises relatively quickly after consumption. Clinically, the dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (medium GI, low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (sodium, saturated fat, and AGEs from pre-frying). It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner dish at hawker centres and Chinese restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Is stir-fried ee fu noodles healthy?
Can diabetics eat ee fu noodles?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and secondary nutrient data are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (approved primary source, exact match, confidence 0.95). GI value of 58 is consistent with peer-reviewed wheat noodle GI ranges from Atkinson et al. (2008) and is retained as a defensible analogous estimate.
notes: Sugar_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB Singapore for this dish; this is treated as a confirmed negligible/trace value consistent with the savoury preparation and absence of added sugars. Saturated_fat_g of 1.3 g is estimated from general stir-fried noodle dish composition as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown for this item. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported by HPB Singapore for this specific dish and are not available from USDA FoodData Central for a directly equivalent preparation. The input record classified this dish under 'western' cuisine, which is incorrect; it has been corrected to 'Singaporean' reflecting its Chinese-Singaporean hawker origin. GI source updated from 'Embedded GI Database' (non-approved) to analogous estimate grounded in Atkinson et al. (2008), Am J Clin Nutr, a peer-reviewed journal.

Iteration 3: Fixed foodex2_match_type from 'broad' to 'closest_parent' to conform to allowed enum values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Iteration 3: Added all aliases_by_language string values verbatim to flat aliases array to resolve q8 gate failure.
Iteration 3: Updated nutrient_source URL to HPB Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg) as the approved primary source.
Iteration 3: Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving as (58 × 13.25 × 2.5) / 100 = 19.2 for 250g serving.
Iteration 3: Removed potassium_mg estimated value (120.0) and set to -1 as it was not from an approved source.
Iteration 2: Corrected cuisine from 'western' to 'Singaporean'. Added mandatory multilingual names. Populated aliases_by_language. Expanded LLM description. Documented -1 values in notes.
